content.full_text:
90-255
JA

LAW OFFICES

OLIVER, STOEVER, BARR & VOS[illegible], [illegible]

THOMAS W. STOEVER
WILLIAM B. BARR
JON F. OLIVER
CONNIE COOKE SANDIFER
ROGER W. SPRINGER
EDWARD W. LEE
HERIBERTO F. DIAZ
JAMES DUFF MURPHY
JANICE R. MIYAHIRA

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
1025 SUNSET BOULEVARD
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012
(213) 250-3043

FB 21
[illegible] [illegible]
TELECOPIER
(818) 482-5336

30

May 21, 1990

FAX

John Rothman, Esq.
Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Request for Advice Letter -
    City of Hermosa Beach

Dear Mr. Rothman:

     This firm represents the City of Hermosa Beach and as the
Assistant City Attorney, I am requesting an advice letter on
behalf of the City related to a conflict of interest of Mayor
Roger Creighton and his possible participation under the City's
General Plan Consistency Program. The material facts are as
follows:

     1.     The City of Hermosa Beach is a general law city
located in the southbay area of the County of Los Angeles. The
City is currently reviewing its General Plan and zoning to
bring the two into consistency as required by Government Code
Section 65860. A certain area of the City (labeled Area 10 and
shown on the site map attached hereto as Exhibit 1) is under
consideration by the City Council in undertaking its
consistency program.

     2.     Area 10 is composed of approximately ten lots. There
are approximately 6,000 lots within the jurisdiction of the
City of Hermosa Beach. The mayor of the City currently owns
and resides in real property which is within 300 feet of the
boundaries of Area 10.

     3.     Area 10 is currently zoned R-2B through a council
action on December 13, 1988 in which the Mayor did not
participate. Area 10 is also designated under the General Plan
as low density residential. The General Plan designation for
low density residential allows 0 to 13 dwelling units per acre;
a medium density designation allows 14 to 25 dwelling units per

OLIVER, STOEVER, BARR & VOSE

John Rothman, Esq.
May 21, 1990
Page 2.

acre.  The current zoning of Area 10 of R-2B allows 16.5
dwelling units per acre, while a change to a R-1 zone would
significantly reduce the density to 9.2 dwelling units per acre
in Area 10.

     4.     On March 21, 1989, the Planning Commission of the
City considered and recommended a redesignation of Area 10
under the City's General Plan from low density to medium
density to bring the General Plan and Zoning into consistently.
Upon consideration by the City Council on July 3, 1989, and
again without the participation of the Mayor, the City Council
considered the recommendation of the Planning Commission.  The
remaining four members of the City Council, after several
consecutive motions, failed to obtain a majority vote on any
motion; each motion resulting in a 2-2 tie vote.  The matter
was referred back to the Planning Commission to consider a zone
change of Area 10 to R-1.

     5.     On September 5, 1989, the Planning Commission again
considered Area 10 and the possible rezoning to R-1 and again
recommended a redesignation from low density to medium density.
The City Council on October 10, 1989, again without the
participation of the Mayor, was unable to resolve the matter
and after several motions again resulted in 2-2 tie voting.
The matter was then continued to another date certain for
Council to reconsider the matter in light of upcoming elections
and a possible change in Council membership.  Mayor Creighton
was not up for re-election.

     6.     On April 10, 1990, the City Council again considered
Area 10 under the Consistency Program, once again without Mayor
Creighton's participation.  The membership of the City Council
had changed and three new Council persons were seated at the
April 10 meeting for reconsideration of Area 10.  However, the
City Council after several consecutive motions to either
redesignate or rezone Area 10 to accomplish consistency, failed
to obtain a majority vote to resolve the matter.

     Based upon the above information, a question has been
raised whether or not the Mayor, under the "Rule of Necessity"
as provided in Section 87101 of the Political Reform Act, can
participate in a vote on Area 10 to achieve consistency as
mandated by the Government Code.  This would include voting on
the rezoning of the subject property to bring it into
compliance with the current general plan or voting on changing
the general plan as it relates to this specific property to

OLIVER, STOEVER, BARR & VOSE

John Rothman, Esq.
May 21, 1990
Page 3.


make it consistent with the existing zoning.  A corollary
question is, assuming the application of the Rule of Necessity,
what is the legally required extent of the Mayor's
participation in the decision-making process?

If you require further facts or information concerning
this matter, kindly contact the undersigned or Charles S. Vose
at this law firm. It would be appreciated if you could furnish
your advice letter prior to the end of June, 1990.

Thank you for your attention and cooperation in this
matter.

Very truly yours,

Edward W. Lee
of OLIVER, STOEVER, BARR & VOSE

EEL:if

CC:  Charles S. Vose, City Attorney
     Kelvin Northcraft, City Manager
     Mayor Roger Creighton and City Councilmembers

sections.question: Based upon the above information, a question has been raised whether or not the Mayor, under the "Rule of Necessity" as provided in Section 87101 of the Political Reform Act, can participate in a vote on Area 10 to achieve consistency as mandated by the Government Code. This would include voting on the rezoning of the subject property to bring it into compliance with the current general plan or voting on changing the general plan as it relates to this specific property to make it consistent with the existing zoning. A corollary question is, assuming the application of the Rule of Necessity, what is the legally required extent of the Mayor's participation in the decision-making process?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Roger Creighton, who owns property within 300 feet of Area 10 under City review, can invoke the "Rule of Necessity" under Political Reform Act Section 87101 to participate in voting on Area 10's zoning and general plan consistency decisions, and if so, to what extent.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or advice section is present. The question section is verbatim from the letter body. Document clearly states factual background about conflict of interest scenario for Mayor participation.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Based upon the above information, a question has been raised whether or not the Mayor, under the "Rule of Necessity" as provided in Section 87101 of the Political Reform Act, can participate in a vote on Area 10 to achieve consistency as mandated by the Government Code. This would include voting on the rezoning of the subject property to bring it into compliance with the current general plan or voting on changing the general plan as it relates to this specific property to make it consistent with the existing zoning. A corollary question is, assuming the application of the Rule of Necessity, what is the legally required extent of the Mayor's participation in the decision-making process?

embedding.summary: A law firm representing the City of Hermosa Beach requests an FPPC advice letter regarding whether Mayor Roger Creighton can invoke the Rule of Necessity to participate in City Council votes on Area 10's zoning and general plan redesignation, given his ownership of property within 300 feet of the subject area.
